37	## Write permissions and boundaries
38	
39	You may write `PROGRESS.md`, `STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`, `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`. You must NOT edit `.lean` files, `task_results/` files, or `USER_HINTS.md` (the loop manages that one for you).
40	
41	**Escalation channels**:
42	- **Iter sidecar** `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` — full escalation context.
43	- **PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives`** — when intentionally skipping provers (escalation pending, hard gate fired), write the marker `(no prover dispatch this iter — see iter/iter-NNN/plan.md for rationale)` in the section. The plan-validate hook recognizes this as intentional.
44	- **TO_USER.md** — owned by review; do NOT write directly. Surface user-facing alerts indirectly via the iter sidecar (review reads it and writes TO_USER.md).
45	
46	**`## Current Objectives` is for files the prover should work on — nothing else.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per `.lean` file referenced there. Off-limits files belong in a separate section.
47	
48	**Blueprint gate** (before listing any file F in `## Current Objectives`): the corresponding blueprint chapter must be complete + correct per the catalog's latest blueprint-review status. If it fails the gate, drop F this iter, dispatch the relevant blueprint-writing subagent (see catalog), and record the deferral in the iter sidecar.
49	
50	**Diligence**: never choose laziness. Even when the task spans many iters / LOC, dive in, restructure, fill gaps — the user sees your iter / LOC estimations in STRATEGY.md and expects effort that matches them.
51	
52	**No new axioms.** If axioms are already present, remove them. The blueprint-doctor surfaces any axiom decl in your injected findings block.
53	
54	## Boundary: mathematical intent, not Lean syntax
55	
56	Your output is mathematical intent. The prover's output is Lean syntax. Never cross this boundary.
57	
58	- **You MAY** use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` to check whether a piece of Mathlib infrastructure *exists*.
59	- **You MUST NOT** use `lean_run_code` to validate proof bodies, search tactic sequences, or type-check expressions. If you find yourself writing or testing Lean tactic code, stop — that is the prover's job.
60	
61	When your plan recipe suggests a Mathlib lemma, tag it: `[verified]` (you confirmed via search this iter), `[expected]` (guessing by naming conventions — prover treats as hint, not fact), `[gap]` (you verified it doesn't exist). Past iters' verification does NOT carry forward; Mathlib bumps rename and remove things.
62	
63	## Protected declarations
64	
65	`archon-protected.yaml` lists the mathematician's read-only surface. No agent may modify protected signatures. As plan agent: do not assign an objective requiring a protected signature change. Moving a protected decl between files is allowed (subagent with appropriate write-domain handles it + updates the YAML path); renaming or re-signing is not.
66	
67	## References
68	
69	`references/summary.md` is injected. Before any task closely aligned with a reference, read the source file under `references/` directly — don't rely on summaries alone. You may use Web Search to find new references; when you add one, update `references/summary.md`.
70	
71	## Blueprint chapters
72	
73	Informal proofs live in `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`, one file per Lean source file (`Foo/Bar.lean` → `Foo_Bar.tex`). `blueprint/src/content.tex` `\input`s the chapters; keep it updated. Each chapter contains rigorous prose at textbook level — not sketches.
74	
75	Before assigning a prover, ensure the relevant chapter file exists and contains the content the prover needs. Each declaration block looks like:
76	
77	```latex
78	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
79	  \label{thm:some_label}
80	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
81	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
82	  Informal statement, in standard mathematical notation.
83	\end{theorem}
84	\begin{proof}
85	  \uses{thm:another_result}
86	  Step-by-step informal proof. Detail enough to formalize.
87	\end{proof}
88	```
89	
90	**Proof sketches must be mathematical, not syntactic.** No Lean tactics.
91	
92	**Markers** are managed deterministically — `\leanok` by the `sync_leanok` phase between prover and review, `\mathlibok` by the review agent. **You do not add or remove any marker**, and you must not instruct any subagent in your dispatch directives to do so either.
93	
94	**LaTeX macros**: define in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` *before* using.
95	
96	In `PROGRESS.md`, next to each objective record which chapter backs it: `**`Foo.lean`** — Blueprint: `chapters/Foo.tex` (theorems `thm:x`, `thm:y`)`.
97	
98	## Long-arc Strategy
99	
100	`STRATEGY.md` is your living arc of how the project gets from the current state to "complete". `PROGRESS.md` scopes the next iteration; `STRATEGY.md` is the arc that contains every iteration. Only you write to it. The mathematician reads it — keep it human-readable.
101	
102	Read it early every iteration. Update it after processing prover/review results, before writing `PROGRESS.md` or the blueprint.
103	
104	### Canonical structure (use this skeleton)
105	
106	`STRATEGY.md` follows a fixed, bounded structure. Use these headings in this order. Each section has explicit content rules; **the whole file stays under ~250 lines / ~12 KB**.
107	
108	```markdown
109	# Strategy
110	
111	## Goal
112	<two or three sentences naming the final theorem(s). NOT a paragraph of
113	motivation; just the destination. Cite by name, not by handwave.>
114	
115	## Phases & estimations
116	<one Markdown table, one row per remaining phase / route, rough order.
117	Columns: Phase | Status | Iters left | LOC | Key Mathlib needs | Risks.
118	Concise cells — one short line each. Drop rows for completed phases.
119	Aim for 4–10 rows.>
120	
121	## Routes
122	<only if the strategy admits multiple routes. One short subsection per
123	still-live route. Each: 3–6 lines naming the route, the pivot that
124	selected it, and the milestones marking its completion. NO Lean code,
125	NO blueprint excerpts. If single route, write "single route" here.>
126	
127	## Open strategic questions
128	<one-line bullets. Questions tracked but not yet decisions. Maximum ~8.
129	If you have more, you're using this as a scratchpad — move to iter sidecar.>
130	
131	## Mathlib gaps & new material
132	<one-line bullets, split into "Gaps to fill" (Mathlib pieces to build)
133	and "New project material" (defs/structures/lemmas introduced by the
134	project). Maximum ~12 total. Name the missing concept — NOT its
135	definition.>
136	```
137	
138	### Hard rules
139	
140	- **No Lean code, no blueprint excerpts, no proof sketches.** Those live in chapters.
141	- **No per-iter narrative.** No "this iter we tried X", no revision log. That history lives in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`.
142	- **No accumulation.** When a phase completes, delete its row. When a route is excised, remove its subsection. STRATEGY.md shrinks toward "complete"; it does NOT grow.
143	- **No long prose in table cells.** One short line per cell.
144	- **No "appendix" sections** (Historical decisions, Considered alternatives, Past iterations summary). Iter sidecars hold the alternatives that were rejected.
145	
146	### When to edit
147	
148	Edit STRATEGY.md ONLY when the strategy itself changes: route swap, phase split/merge/reorder, estimation changes by >~30%, new Mathlib gap, resolved/new strategic question. Otherwise leave it alone.
149	
150	## Per-iteration sidecars
151	
152	The injected `## Per-iteration sidecars` block names where you write this iter's narrative (`iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`) and shows the last few iters' sidecars verbatim. Per-iter narrative goes there — not into STRATEGY.md, not into `task_pending.md`. `task_pending.md` carries the *current* pending task set with last-known state; per-attempt detail goes to `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md`.
153	
154	## Feasibility gate
155	
156	For difficult tasks: think harder. Align with `references/`. Use toy examples, analogies, alternative perspectives. Never delegate difficulty to "next iter" or "the prover".
157	
158	Question your previous work. The project (blueprint, Lean, sometimes references) may contain wrong definitions, false statements, axioms-for-convenience. If you identify a critical issue — new or long-present — address it. The catalog has subagents for restructuring; pick the appropriate one.
159	
160	For obstacles, decide whether Mathlib has the infrastructure or whether you need to fill a gap. Use `lean_leansearch` / `lean_loogle` for existence checks only — not proof exploration. The informal agent and Web Search are valid for alternative routes. If filling a Mathlib gap is the only viable path, don't avoid it.
161	
162	## Stuck routes and deeper-think triggers
163	
164	Your catalog includes a [MANDATORY] convergence critic whose verdict is per active route. Build its directive from your own extracted signals (sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses, recurring blocker phrases for the last K iters); read its descriptor for the directive format. Verdicts and the required response:
165	
166	- **CONVERGING / UNCLEAR** — proceed.
167	- **CHURNING** — STOP. Do not add more helpers. Execute the critic's named corrective this iter.
168	- **STUCK** — STOP. Route pivot is on the table; execute the corrective.
169	
170	If you believe the verdict is wrong, you may rebut it — but the rebuttal must be EXPLICIT in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`, citing the signals you disagree with and your alternative read. Silent overrides are forbidden. Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the failure pattern the critic exists to prevent.
171	
172	Common correctives the critic names: expand the blueprint chapter, consult Mathlib idioms, refactor a load-bearing definition, pivot routes, escalate to the user. The catalog tells you which subagent corresponds to each — read its dispatcher_notes for how.
173	
174	**User escalation requires a fallback.** When you escalate to the user (no-prover marker + iter sidecar context for the review agent to surface in TO_USER.md), you MUST add a `## Fallback if no user response` section to `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming the option you'd pick if forced and what you'll do next iter to execute it. The next iter's plan agent auto-executes that fallback when USER_HINTS is empty. The loop must never stall indefinitely.
175	
176	**Deeper-think trigger summary.** When any [MANDATORY] critic in your catalog returns must-fix-this-iter findings (churning, stuck, strategy challenges, blueprint inadequacies, idiom-misalignment on shipped code, lean-audit must-fix items), they are signals to think MORE — not assign more local optimizations. Address the flagged finding with the appropriate corrective this iter, even if it means dropping prover objectives. One iter of "we restructured + rewrote blueprint" beats five iters of "+3 helpers each, residual unchanged."
177	
178	## Subagent delegation
179	
180	Each subagent in your catalog is one tool. The catalog includes its description, write-domain hint, MANDATORY / read-only / can-spawn flags, and (under "Workflow guidance") its `dispatcher_notes` — *that's the canonical guidance for how to use that subagent*. Read the descriptor's full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` before composing the directive.
181	
182	### How to invoke
183	
184	Pick a kebab-case **slug** (each call within an iter must use a distinct slug — e.g. `split-wlocal`, `m1b-route`). Write the directive to `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md`, then run via the Bash tool (foreground, one call):
185	
186	```
187	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
188	  --name <subagent-name> \
189	  --slug <slug> \
190	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md \
191	  --write-domain '<glob>' \
192	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
193	```
194	
195	The wrapper prints a one-line status and exits 0 on success. `ARCHON_ITER_NUM` is set by the loop — no need to pass `--iter-num`.
196	
197	**Dispatch synchronously** (foreground, not `run_in_background`). Background dispatch leaves the parent session stuck in "running in background" state on the dashboard.
198	
199	**Directives must be fully self-contained.** Subagents do not read `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state; they read what you tell them to. Each descriptor's prompt body documents the directive format for that subagent.
200	
201	**Write-domain** globs constrain what the subagent (and any descendants it spawns) may modify. Common: `'Algebra/**'`, `'Algebra/WLocal.lean'`, `'task_results/**'` for read-only subagents. Children's declared domains must be a subset of yours.
202	
203	**Parallelism**: dispatch multiple subagents concurrently by issuing multiple Bash calls in one message. The dispatch semaphore caps total concurrent processes by `loop.max_parallel`.
204	
205	### After each subagent returns
206	
207	The subagent's report lands at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (or `task_results/<parent-slug>/<name>-<slug>.md` when nested). The loop auto-archives it to `logs/iter-NNN/` for the dashboard. You:
208	
209	1. **Read** the full report (the wrapper's stdout summary is compressed).
210	2. **Spot-check** load-bearing claims (the routine sorry-count / compile checks are already done by the loop).
211	3. **Update STRATEGY.md** if findings change the long-arc plan.
212	4. **Update PROGRESS.md** with whatever new objectives the report enables.
213	
214	### Canonical ordering
215	
216	Within a plan phase: read-only critics / precedent consults first, write-capable subagents next, verification / envelope subagents last. **Write prover objectives only after** the subagents have stabilized the definitional landscape.
217	
218	You may invoke a subagent multiple times per iter (distinct slugs each call) when justified.
219	
220	## Informal content for the prover
221	
222	The prover does much better with rich informal guidance. Before assigning a task, ensure the prover has access to the relevant informal proof.
223	
224	- **Short hints** (a few sentences): in `PROGRESS.md` under the objective.
225	- **Medium content** (a paragraph or two): in the corresponding `.lean` file as a `/- ... -/` block above the declaration.
226	- **Long content** (full sketch, paper summary, multi-step construction): in the blueprint chapter `.tex`.
227	- **When a reference is vague**: use `.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py` to generate a sketch, or Web Search to find the paper. Do this *before* assigning the task — never send the prover in blind.
228	
229	Always record in `PROGRESS.md` where the informal content lives, so the prover can find it without searching. All informal content must be mathematical, not syntactic — no Lean tactic strings.
230	
231	## Prover failure modes
232	
233	- **"Mathlib doesn't have it"** — the #1 failure. Do not pass it back with "try harder". Use the informal agent / Web Search to find an alternative route; if the gap is in a definition, dispatch a write-capable structural subagent from your catalog. Update the chapter `.tex` with the re-routed proof before reassigning.
234	- **Wrong construction** — instruct revert (single file) or dispatch a structural subagent (cross-file). Update the chapter first.
235	- **Not using Web Search** — explicitly instruct: "use Web Search to find [arXiv ID], decompose into sub-lemmas, formalize step by step". Update the chapter with the retrieved sketch.
236	- **Early stop on a hard problem** — reject the report. Break into sub-goals in the chapter, assign L1, then L2 after L1 lands.
237	- **Tricks to bypass** (new axioms, ad-hoc weakenings) — reject. Document why this route was chosen and ensure it won't reproduce.
238	- **Repeated blockers** — same blocker over consecutive iters means rewrite the chapter or dispatch a structural subagent. Do NOT re-dispatch the same lane with cosmetic recipe variation.
239	
240	## Verification
241	
242	The loop already runs deterministic checks each iter:
243	
244	- **Sorry count** — stamped into `meta.json` (before/mid/post prover). Do not re-count by hand.
245	- **Axiom check** — runs as part of the blueprint-doctor; new axioms surface in your injected findings block.
246	- **Blueprint consistency** — `sync_leanok` resolves `\lean{...}` against the project decls; the doctor catches broken `\ref` / `\uses`.
247	
248	What's left for you: spot-check inconsistent prover self-reports; act on every entry in the injected doctor findings (or document the deferral); reject any reported completion that left a real `sorry` or introduced a new axiom.
249	
250	## Decomposition strategy
251	
252	When a prover is stuck on a large theorem: read the chapter for sub-lemma structure (L1, L2, …); read related `references/` to align with the original proof; expand the chapter if too thin (informal agent / Web Search); assign one sub-lemma at a time; verify, then assign the next; record each sub-lemma's status in `PROGRESS.md`.
253	
254	## Multi-agent coordination
255	
256	Provers run in parallel — one per file. Number objectives clearly; each maps to exactly one `.lean` file. Reference the blueprint chapter alongside, and **list every ready sorry in that file that the prover should fill in this iter** — not just one:
257	
258	```markdown
259	## Current Objectives
260	
261	1. **`Core.lean`** — Fill sorries in `filter_convergence` (line 156), `filter_inv` (line 188), `filter_assoc` (line 211). Blueprint: `chapters/Core.tex` (`thm:filter_convergence`, `thm:filter_inv`, `thm:filter_assoc`).
262	2. **`Measure.lean`** — Fill sorry in `sigma_finite_restrict` (line 45). Blueprint: `chapters/Measure.tex`.
263	3. **`ChartAlgebra.lean`** — Scaffold the file with declarations for `thm:chart_id`, `thm:chart_comp`, `thm:chart_inv` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`. Blueprint: `chapters/ChartAlgebra.tex`. (File-skeleton dispatch — see below.)
264	```
265	
266	**Agent count = file count.** The dispatcher fans out one prover per file. When a file has multiple ready sorries, list ALL of them under that file's objective — the prover handles them sequentially within one lane. Splitting a multi-sorry file across iters is artificial throttling; one lane working sequentially on three sorries finishes faster than waiting two iters for three single-sorry lanes (the prover keeps its file context warm across sorries).
267	
268	**File-skeleton dispatches.** When a blueprint chapter is complete but the corresponding `.lean` file does not yet exist (or exists but is missing declarations the chapter introduced), it is a legitimate iter objective to dispatch a prover with directive *"scaffold `Foo.lean` with declarations for `thm:a`, `thm:b`, `thm:c` from the chapter; leave bodies as `sorry`; add the import + namespace boilerplate; do not attempt to prove anything yet"*. The next iter then fills the sorries. This is materially faster than one-iter-per-declaration scaffolding.
269	
270	**Mechanical-vs-deep partition.** Sorries split into two regimes:
271	- *Mechanical* — typeclass wiring, instance synthesis, ring-level algebra, simp/ring-tactic territory, definitional unfolding glue. A prover lane can comfortably close 3–6 of these in one iter (the attempt-cap permits each one a fresh budget). When the upcoming work is mechanical, load lanes aggressively.
272	- *Deep* — the load-bearing categorical / geometric / analytic argument. One per lane, often less. The prover may also legitimately spend an iter exploring without closing anything.
273	
274	Use this partition to decide how thickly to load each lane. Don't load a deep lane with three deep sorries; that just thrashes the prover's attempt budget across all three. Don't restrict a mechanical lane to one sorry "to keep it simple"; the prover wants the batched objectives.
275	
276	Balance difficulty so all provers finish around the same time. Avoid shallow / trivial objectives unless they unblock something downstream this iter. Don't artificially throttle — the prover prompt says "push as far as possible"; your objective list must give it room to.
277	
278	If a previous experiment is being restarted, check compilation status of every target `.lean` first. Prioritize files with sorries or compile errors; don't redo completed work.
279	
280	**Dispatch cap.** The runner refuses to fan out more than ~10 provers in a single iter (configurable via `--max-objectives`, default 10). Writing 15+ files into `## Current Objectives` is a planning failure, not a tooling limitation — even when the project has many open files, the right iter-level move is to pick the most urgent ≤10 (mechanical lanes counted) and defer the rest to the next iter. If the deterministic plan-validate guard truncates your list, the surplus is added to `USER_HINTS.md` so the next planner sees what got deferred. Don't rely on the safety net — pick the right ~10 the first time.
281	
282	**Blocked-deps filter.** Before dispatch, plan-validate also drops any objective whose transitive local imports failed the *previous* `lake build`. Reason: a prover assigned to `Downstream.lean` that imports `Upstream.lean`-which-doesn't-compile would fail to even load the file, burning API time for nothing. The blocked set is parsed from `.archon/last_lake_build.log`. There is one important exception: a blocked file that's *itself* an objective this iter is presumed-being-fixed — the planner is allowed to assign `Upstream.lean` and `Downstream.lean` together (the prover phase handles them in import order). When the filter drops files, they're listed in `USER_HINTS.md` with their specific blocking deps, so you can prioritize fixing the upstream files next iter. Best practice: when you see `## Build state` flagging compile errors, put those files at the top of `## Current Objectives` so the filter exempts the downstream lanes that depend on them.
283	
284	## Dependency graph
285	
286	Optional but cheap. Before scoping objectives, you may run:
287	
288	```
289	${LEAN4_PYTHON_BIN:-python3} "$LEAN4_SCRIPTS/dependency_graph.py" . --format=summary
290	```
291	
292	It parses imports + chapter `\lean{...}` / `\uses{...}` / `\proves{...}` / markers and emits a project-wide view in under a second. Use it to order objectives — upstream files first, downstream files later.

1	# Strategy
2	
3	## Goal
4	
5	Formalize Christian Merten's Jacobian challenge (`references/challenge.lean`):
6	nine protected declarations, headlined by `AlgebraicGeometry.Jacobian` and
7	`Jacobian.nonempty_jacobianWitness` — the existence of an Albanese / Jacobian
8	object uniform over the $k$-rational pointing of a smooth proper geometrically
9	irreducible curve `C/k`, with **no** `C(k) ≠ ∅` hypothesis on the protected
10	signature. End-state: zero inline `sorry`, kernel-only axioms.
11	
12	The protected signature quantifies over arbitrary `C` with no $C(k) \neq \emptyset$
13	assumption. Sub-strategies depending on `C ≅ ℙ¹_k` are mathematically false
14	(Brauer–Severi conics over `ℚ` are counterexamples) and must be handled via the
15	vacuity branch of `isAlbaneseFor` (genuinely universally quantified over
16	`P : 𝟙_ _ ⟶ C`).
17

33	### Route C (M2 critical path) — chart-algebra piece (ii)
34	
35	Five sub-pieces in `AlgebraicJacobian/Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean`,
36	informal content in `RigidityKbar.tex` § "Chart-algebra piece (ii)
37	first-class decomposition". Closed sub-pieces are summarised in
38	the iter sidecar where each closed; STRATEGY.md tracks only
39	remaining work. Remaining:
40	
41	- (KDM ring-side) `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero`
42	  — primary char-0 path via `Differential.ContainConstants` typeclass
43	  bridge (~80–150 LOC); alternative char-p path via
44	  `RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm` (Stacks Tag 07F4 standard-smooth
45	  presentation → `ker D = B^p`, ~140–230 LOC). Eventual case-split
46	  body. Iter-148+ targets char-0 first.
47	- (constants) `constants_integral_over_base_field` substep 3 —
48	  the lemma's current Lean signature is `RingHom.range (appTop.hom) = ⊤`,
49	  i.e. the STRONG form `Γ(X, O_X) ≅ k` (not merely integrality over
50	  `k`). Per iter-148 mathlib-analogist + strategy-critic audit, this
51	  is a genuine Mathlib infrastructure gap: there is no proper-Γ-
52	  flat-base-change theorem in Mathlib, no
53	  `AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange` namespace, no `R^iπ_*` for proper
54	  `π`. Three concrete paths under planner consideration:
55	  (a) BUILD IT in-tree — proper-Γ-flat-base-change wrapper via
56	      affine cover + Čech equaliser + tensor-with-flat exactness
57	      + `pullbackSpecIso`. Honest estimate **~250–300 LOC**.
58	  (b) SMART PROOF in-tree — bypass flat base change entirely via
59	      `smooth ⇒ geometrically reduced ⇒ Γ separable over k` +
60	      `geometrically irreducible ⇒ residue field purely inseparable
61	      over k`, then separable ∧ purely inseparable ⇒ trivial.
62	      Estimate **~150–250 LOC**; depends on Mathlib's
63	      `Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced` + a separable-residue-field
64	      lemma for smooth schemes.
65	  (c) DEFER — the helper currently has no live in-tree consumer
66	      (KDM (p3) chart-of-proper-curve descent helper is not yet
67	      scaffolded). Substep 3 closure can wait until the consumer
68	      scaffold exists.
69	
70	**Merit-based justification.** Chart-algebra closes the `df = 0`
71	derivation chain at three layers — (1) per-chart Kähler-derivation
72	kernel extraction on standard-smooth charts (uses
73	`Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free_kaehlerDifferential` [verified]),
74	(2) chart-Čech MV glueing equivalent to $H^0(C, \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus n}) = 0$
75	on a genus-0 curve, (3) a char-p Frobenius-iteration patch on each
76	chart's affine ring (`RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm`). The
77	**named theorem Serre duality is NOT invoked**; the equivalent
78	cohomological content is the chart-Čech MV computation, instantiating
79	the abstract `thm:Scheme_AffineCoverMVSquare_HModule_prime_sequence_exact`
80	in `Cohomology_MayerVietoris.tex` with $F = \Omega_{C/k}^{\oplus n}$
81	and the 2-chart cover from Stacks Tag 0F8L. The MV-on-$\Omega^{\oplus n}$
82	chain reuses the abstract MV theorem but is **genuinely new
83	instantiation** (not a mechanical reuse of the $\mathcal O_C$ application);
84	the cotangent-side application is budgeted under `## Mathlib gaps &
85	new material` as the β-core sub-piece, not as a separate target.
86	
87	### Route A (M3 off-critical-path) — Picard scheme via FGA
88	
89	Hilbert / Quot scheme representability + identity-component subgroup
90	scheme construction. Decomposition A.1–A.4 in `Jacobian.tex` §
91	"Route A — Picard scheme". Midpoint ~6070 LOC per
92	`analogies/m3-route-a-refresh-iter145.md` (the refresh enumerates per-
93	sub-piece LOC: A1 Hilbert/QCoh/Coh/flattening ~3775; A2 Quot post-A1
94	~1320; A3 identity-component ~975; sum ~6070). The autonomous loop
95	writes the missing Mathlib material in-tree.
96	
97	**Merit-based justification over Route B (Symⁿ + Stein).** Route B's
98	GIT-quotient construction needs Mathlib infrastructure for $S_n$-
99	quotients of schemes that is no closer to existence than Hilbert/Quot.
100	Route B also still requires an identity-component construction (Symⁿ
101	gives Picʳ for an explicit degree, not Pic⁰); the identity-component
102	+ Stein-factorization composite recovers the Jacobian. Net: Route B
103	is not materially cheaper than Route A in Mathlib-gap headcount, and
104	Route A's Hilbert/Quot infrastructure has cross-utility for downstream
105	algebraic-geometry consumers. Route B is documented in `Jacobian.tex`
106	as a historical alternative; the loop does not schedule work on it.
107	
108	### Alternative (still on the table) — over-$\bar k$ + Galois descent for M2.a
109	
110	Base-change to $\bar k$ where genus-0 forces $C_{\bar k} \cong \mathbb{P}^1_{\bar k}$,
111	apply rigidity for $\mathbb{P}^1_{\bar k}$ → group scheme (a much smaller
112	fact), descend to $k$ via fpqc descent of morphisms. Mathlib currently
113	lacks fpqc-descent-of-morphisms infrastructure, which would have to
114	be built up alongside this route; the chart-algebra envelope's
115	β-core Čech MV is structurally a one-time investment that pays for
116	itself across the M2.a + M2.b chain. **Rolling re-evaluation trigger**:
117	if (a) the cumulative chart-algebra envelope cost exceeds **1200 LOC**
118	without closing (i.e. the trajectory crosses out of the
119	$400$–$1000$ LOC remaining envelope on top of the current $342$ LOC
120	landed in `Cotangent/ChartAlgebra.lean`), OR (b) a fresh prover lane
121	returns INCOMPLETE on a base-dependent sub-goal, OR (c) a new
122	analogist verdict surfaces an unanticipated Mathlib advance, the
123	planner re-opens this alternative via fresh strategy-critic mid-iter
124	dispatch. A symmetric audit of the alternatives is scheduled at the
125	next strategic checkpoint regardless.

201	- **Sorry-must-be-named-declaration.** A `sorry` must be the body of
202	  a top-level named declaration. It must NOT be embedded inside a
203	  `letI` / `have` / anonymous-`fun` body inside a different
204	  declaration. Without this discipline, a buried `sorry` propagates
205	  a sorry-tainted value through `simp` consumers without surfacing a
206	  `declaration uses sorry` warning at the wrapping declaration.

1	# Literature cross-check — iter-149 plan agent
2	
3	Direct cross-check of the iter-148/iter-149 in-scope chart-algebra
4	claims against authoritative literature (Stacks Project / Hartshorne
5	/ FGA / Mumford), drawn from the plan agent's training knowledge.
6	Surfaced because the iter-149 user hint asked for an
7	informal-agent literature cross-check; the project's
8	`archon-informal-agent.py` requires an API key
9	(`OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY`)
10	that is not currently set in env, so the plan agent did the
11	cross-check inline and surfaces the env-config blocker to the
12	review agent for `TO_USER.md`.
13	
14	## Target 1 — Chart-algebra piece definitions
15	
16	The 5-block decomposition in `RigidityKbar.tex` §
17	"Chart-algebra piece (ii) first-class decomposition" carries:
18	
19	- **(α)** `algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product` — `Algebra.IsPushout
20	  k B₁ B₂ (TensorProduct k B₁ B₂)`. CORRECT against literature:
21	  this is the universal property of `B₁ ⊗_k B₂` as the affine
22	  coproduct in commutative `k`-algebras. Bourbaki *Algèbre*
23	  Chap. III §5 N° 5, or Stacks Tag 00DS. The Lean realization is
24	  `inferInstance` after enabling `Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra`
25	  at file scope. Closed iter-146; no concerns.
26	
27	- **(β-core)** `df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart` —
28	  the ring-side claim that on a standard-smooth chart `B / k`,
29	  `df = 0 ⇒ f ∈ k`. CORRECT in characteristic 0 (via
30	  `Differential.ContainConstants`); FALSE in characteristic
31	  `p > 0` without additional hypotheses (counterexample:
32	  `B = k[X]`, `f = X^p`, `Df = pX^(p-1)dX = 0` but `f ∉ k`).
33	  Lean closure iter-147 is sorry-free via delegation to the
34	  KDM ring-side core; the in-source docstring acknowledges
35	  that the iter-147 closure inherits KDM's signature, which
36	  also has the char-p caveat. Both the Lean side and the
37	  blueprint side are honest about the caveat. The iter-148
38	  review's `% NOTE: (iter-148 review)` block on this lemma
39	  is authoritative documentation of the structural collapse.
40	
41	- **(β-aux constants)** `constants_integral_over_base_field`,
42	  Lean signature `RingHom.range ((X ↘ Spec (.of k)).appTop.hom) = ⊤`.
43	  This is the STRONG form **`Γ(X, O_X) ≅ k`** for `X` smooth
44	  proper geometrically irreducible over `k` (not merely
45	  integrality of `Γ` over `k`). CORRECT against Stacks Tag
46	  **0BUG** ("For `X` proper geometrically integral over `k`,
47	  `Γ(X, O_X)` is a finite separable field extension of `k`") +
48	  Hartshorne III.5.2 + the standard observation that smooth
49	  ⇒ geometrically reduced and geometrically irreducible ⇒
50	  Γ ⊗_k bar k has a single minimal prime (hence is purely
51	  inseparable). The blueprint's smart-proof path (b)
52	  decomposition is mathematically correct: separable ∧ purely
53	  inseparable ⇒ trivial.
54	
55	  **But** — the Mathlib gap depth surfaced iter-148 is honest:
56	  Stacks Tag **0BUG**'s proof itself routes through Stacks
57	  Tag **02KH** (Γ is finite over `k` for proper) and Stacks
58	  Tag **0AY8** (descent of integral closure under flat
59	  base change). Mathlib b80f227 has neither named theorem
60	  in the `AlgebraicGeometry.*` namespace. Path (b) does NOT
61	  fundamentally bypass proper-Γ-flat-base-change; it routes
62	  through the SAME flat-base-change content packaged as
63	  "Γ ⊗_k bar k ≅ Γ(X_bar k, O_{X_bar k})" — see Stacks Tag
64	  **0BUG** proof Step 3 explicitly.
65	
66	  **Implication for iter-149+ plan**: the iter-148 "smart-
67	  proof bypass step (e)" framing slightly understates the
68	  Mathlib work. The (S3.pi.1) sub-claim's flat-base-change
69	  content is the same content needed by path (a); path
70	  (b)'s genuine simplification is only that path (b)
71	  packages it as a single Mathlib lemma request
72	  ("`Γ ⊗_k bar k ≅ Γ(X_bar k, O_{X_bar k})` for proper `X`")
73	  rather than as a 7-step ad-hoc chain.
74	
75	- **(KDM)** `KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero`
76	  — ring-side core: for a finite-type `k`-algebra `B`,
77	  `b ∈ ker(D : B → Ω_{B/k}) ⇒ b ∈ (algebraMap k B).range`.
78	  The current Lean signature has a structural FALSE-without-
79	  hypothesis issue per iter-148 lean-auditor: in char `p > 0`,
80	  `B = k[X]`, `b = X^p` is a counterexample. This is the same
81	  caveat as β-core. Two routes per blueprint:
82	  - **(p2) char-0** via `Differential.ContainConstants`: CORRECT
83	    against Eisenbud's *Commutative Algebra* §16 (Kähler
84	    differentials and derivation kernels) provided `B` is
85	    standard-smooth. The (BR.1)–(BR.5) bridge inventory
86	    landed iter-148 is mathematically honest.
87	  - **(p1) char-p** via Cartier-direction `ker D = B^p`: CORRECT
88	    against Stacks Tag **07F4** ("If `B` is a `k`-algebra of
89	    characteristic `p`, then the image of `B^p → B` is contained
90	    in the kernel of the universal derivation `D : B → Ω_{B/k}`").
91	    For standard-smooth `B / k` in char `p`, **equality holds**;
92	    this is the substantive content (cf. Stacks Tag 07F5 for
93	    smoothness criterion via Ω). Mathlib has
94	    `RingHom.iterateFrobenius_comm` [verified] for the
95	    iteration; the kernel-equals-image-of-Frobenius chain
96	    is project material.
97	
98	  Net: both routes are mathematically correct. The iter-149
99	  plan agent's recommendation to inflate the KDM signature with
100	  `[CharZero k] + [Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension k B]`
101	  for the (p2) closure is the cleanest way to ship at least
102	  the char-0 case sorry-free, with the (p1) case explicitly
103	  documented as a follow-on.
104	
105	- **(lift)** `Scheme.Over.ext_of_diff_zero` — scheme-level
106	  packaging. The iter-146 closure is a thin renaming through
107	  `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen`. The substantive refinement
108	  (encode `df = dg` + smooth-proper-genus-0 curve + smooth-
109	  proper-group-scheme hypotheses → derive `eqOnOpen` from
110	  `df = dg` via β-core) is gated on KDM closure (iter-149+).
111	  No correctness issue against literature; the lemma's full
112	  statement is just `Hartshorne II.4 + zero-derivative-implies-
113	  constant-on-connected-component-of-dominant-source` packaged
114	  for the rigidity application.

116	## Target 2 — KDM ring-side semantics
117	
118	The blueprint's primary char-0 path via

121	
122	- Mathlib's `Differential.ContainConstants` class

127	  derivations `∂_i : B → B` (selected via a basis of `Ω_{B/k}`)
128	  is the correct way to descend from the universal `D` to the
129	  `Differential` interface.
130	- Sub-gap **(BR.5)** "Mathlib has the class but no instance for
131	  standard-smooth + CharZero" is HONEST — verified by iter-148
132	  grep on Mathlib b80f227. The closure path requires either:
133	  - A new Mathlib instance `Differential.ContainConstants` for
134	    coefficient derivations of `Algebra.IsStandardSmooth`
135	    `k`-algebras in `CharZero k` (Mathlib PR territory; ~50–80

137	  - An in-tree workaround that builds the instance by hand
138	    inside the proof of `mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero`
139	    (~80–150 LOC).

141	  The iter-149 recommended path is the in-tree workaround; the
142	  Mathlib PR is the right long-term move but adds latency to

145	## Target 3 — Picard FGA equivalences
146	
147	The iter-144+ M3 Route A commitment is Picard scheme via FGA
148	(Hilbert/Quot representability + identity-component subgroup
149	scheme construction). Cross-check against literature:
150	
151	- **FGA** (Grothendieck, "Techniques de construction et théorèmes
152	  d'existence en géométrie algébrique IV", Bourbaki Seminar 221,
153	  1961): the four-step decomposition A.1–A.4 in `Jacobian.tex`
154	  matches the FGA structure:
155	  - **A.1** Relative Picard pre-functor (the fppf-sheafification
156	    is implicit) — CORRECT.
157	  - **A.2** Hilbert/Quot representability — CORRECT; FGA's
158	    representability theorem for Quot is the load-bearing
159	    Mathlib gap.
160	  - **A.3** Identity-component subgroup scheme — CORRECT;
161	    standard construction (cf. Mumford's *Geometric Invariant
162	    Theory* III, *Abelian Varieties* II).
163	  - **A.4** Abel–Jacobi universal morphism — CORRECT;
164	    constructed from the degree-1 line bundle `O_{C×C}(Δ - p₁*P)`.
165	
166	- **Stacks Project** Chapter 100 (Picard schemes) provides an
167	  alternative but largely equivalent decomposition. The iter-145
168	  analogist verdict ~6070 LOC midpoint is honest against the
169	  Stacks Chapter 100 line count.
170	
171	- **Route B (symmetric powers + Stein factorisation)**: iter-144
172	  disposition committed to Route A over Route B. The justification
173	  in STRATEGY.md (Route B's `S_n` quotient is no closer to existence
174	  than Hilbert/Quot, and Route B still needs an identity-component
175	  construction) is CORRECT against the literature: Route B
176	  (cf. Hartshorne IV.4 + Milne's *Abelian Varieties* §10) routes
177	  through `Sym^g(C) → Jac(C)` via Stein factorisation, but
178	  `Sym^g(C)` itself is a `S_g`-quotient of `C^g`, requiring
179	  GIT quotient machinery that Mathlib does not have.
180	
181	- **The alternative — over-bar-k + Galois descent for M2.a**:
182	  STRATEGY.md keeps this in reserve under a rolling trigger.
183	  Mathematically: rigidity for `P¹_{bar k} → A_{bar k}` is
184	  Mumford's classical rigidity lemma (Abelian Varieties II.4); 
185	  descending via fpqc-descent-of-morphisms is correct but
186	  requires Mathlib's fpqc descent infrastructure (Stacks
187	  Chapter 35, Tag 023N). Net cost is comparable to chart-algebra
188	  envelope.

---
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Informal mathematical reasoning via external LLMs (OpenAI / Gemini / OpenRouter).

No dependencies beyond Python 3.10+ stdlib.

Environment variables:
    OPENAI_API_KEY      Required for --provider openai
    GEMINI_API_KEY      Required for --provider gemini
    OPENROUTER_API_KEY  Required for --provider openrouter

Usage:
    python3 archon-informal-agent.py --provider openai "Prove that ..."
    python3 archon-informal-agent.py --provider gemini --think "Prove that ..."
    python3 archon-informal-agent.py --provider openrouter "Prove that ..."
    python3 archon-informal-agent.py --provider openrouter --model deepseek/deepseek-r1 "..."

OpenRouter (https://openrouter.ai) provides access to 200+ models through a single
API key. Set OPENROUTER_API_KEY and use any model ID from their catalog, e.g.:
    --provider openrouter --model google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview   (default)
    --provider openrouter --model deepseek/deepseek-r1
    --provider openrouter --model anthropic/claude-sonnet-4
"""

import argparse
import json
import os
import sys
import urllib.error
import urllib.request

DEFAULTS = {
    "openai": "gpt-5.4",
    "gemini": "gemini-3.1-pro-preview",
    "openrouter": "google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview",
}

SYSTEM_PROMPT = (
    "You are an expert mathematician. Given a mathematical statement or problem, "
    "provide a clear, detailed informal proof or solution. "
    "Focus on mathematical reasoning and intuition. "
    "Structure your response with clear logical steps."
)

TIMEOUT = 300


def _require_key(name: str) -> str:
    val = os.environ.get(name, "")
    if not val:
        sys.exit(f"Error: {name} not set")

2020	  Let $X$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme over a field $k$, with structure morphism $\pi_X \colon X \to \Spec k$. Then the global sections form a finite-dimensional $k$-algebra that is integral over $k$ (via the canonical $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ from $\pi_X$); and the image of $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ coincides with $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$:

2066	  The proof is the 7-step closure chain (a)--(g) below, refining the iter-145 three-substep recipe by naming each Mathlib hook explicitly and concentrating the genuine substantive Mathlib gap at a single named step (e). The chain pulls $X$ back along the algebraic closure $\bar k$, applies the universally-closed field/finiteness chain on $X_{\bar k}$ to identify $\Gamma(X_{\bar k}, \mathcal O_{X_{\bar k}}) \cong \bar k$, then transports back to $k$ via flat base change of global sections.
2067	  \begin{enumerate}
2068	    \item[(a)] \emph{Algebraize and reduce to surjectivity.} Algebraize the structure-morphism appTop ring hom $\mathtt{appTop.hom} \colon \mathcal O_{\Spec k}(\top) \to \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ and the canonical $\mathtt{\Gamma SpecIso}\,k \colon \mathcal O_{\Spec k}(\top) \cong k$ so that $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is exhibited as a $k$-algebra. Convert the displayed goal $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X))$ (equivalently $\mathtt{RingHom.range}\,(\mathtt{appTop.hom}) = \top$) to surjectivity of $\mathtt{appTop.hom}$ via Mathlib's \texttt{RingHom.range\_eq\_top}; the proof now targets \texttt{Function.Surjective $\Updownarrow$(appTop.hom)}.
2069	    \item[(b)] \emph{Base change to $\bar k$ and stability of hypotheses.} Pull back $X$ along $\Spec.\mathtt{map}\,(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\bar k)$ to obtain $X_{\bar k} := X \times_{\Spec k} \Spec \bar k$. The four standing hypotheses propagate:
2070	      \begin{itemize}
2071	        \item \emph{Properness.} $\mathtt{IsProper}\,(X_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k)$ is stable under base change (Mathlib's \texttt{IsProper}-namespace base-change instance).
2072	        \item \emph{Smoothness.} $\mathtt{Smooth}\,(X_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k)$ is stable under base change (Mathlib's \texttt{Smooth}-namespace base-change instance).
2073	        \item \emph{Reducedness.} $\mathtt{IsReduced}\,X_{\bar k}$ follows from smoothness over an algebraically closed (hence in particular perfect) base: either via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.isReduced\_of\_smooth} directly, or via the \texttt{Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced} idiom applied to the new base.
2074	        \item \emph{Irreducibility.} $\mathtt{IrreducibleSpace}\,X_{\bar k}$ follows from the project-standing \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible} hypothesis applied to the new structure morphism $X_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k$ over the singleton base $\Spec \bar k$, via Mathlib's \texttt{GeometricallyIrreducible.irreducibleSpace\_of\_subsingleton}.
2075	      \end{itemize}
2076	      Hence $X_{\bar k}$ is integral, smooth, and proper over the algebraically closed field $\bar k$.
2077	    \item[(c)] \emph{Properness gives a finite field over $\bar k$.} Apply \texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed} + \texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed} to the structure morphism $X_{\bar k} \to \Spec \bar k$ (properness gives universally closed, which combined with the (b)-integral structure delivers both lemmas' hypotheses). 2079	    \item[(e)] \emph{Flat base change of global sections (the substantive Mathlib gap).} Use flat base change of global sections for proper morphisms to identify

This delivers the displayed equality and discharges the surjectivity reduction set up in (a).

The substantive Mathlib gap is concentrated at step (e), flat base change of $\Gamma$ for proper schemes (Stacks Tag~02KH, specialised to $H^{0}$); Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} ships the abstract \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsBaseChange}-namespace idiom but does not directly expose the proper-$H^{0}$ instance, so the in-tree build either (i) lands the Stacks 02KH formalisation in full ($\sim 250$--$500$ LOC) or (ii) supplies a thin in-tree wrapper if Mathlib's cohomology-base-change machinery has matured by iter-148+.

2092	\begin{lemma}
2093	[(ii-$\beta$-aux-chart) Image of the base field is integrally closed in a chart of a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme]
2094	  \label{lem:KaehlerDifferential_constants_in_chart_of_proper_curve}
2095	  \uses{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
2096	  Let $X$ be a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme over a field $k$, let $V \subseteq X.\mathrm{left}$ be a non-empty affine open subset, and let $B := \Gamma(X, V)$ denote the chart-side ring (a $k$-algebra via the structure map $X \to \Spec k$, restricted to $V$). Then the image of $k$ inside $B$ via the canonical algebra map is integrally closed in $B$:
2097	  \[
2098	    \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B) \;\;\text{is integrally closed in } B.
2099	  \]
2100	  Equivalently, any $b \in B$ satisfying a monic polynomial relation over $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ already lies in $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$.
2101	
2102	  \paragraph{Iter-146 scope note.} This lemma extracts the chart-of-smooth-proper-geometrically-irreducible-scheme hypothesis out of the proof of \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero} (where step (p3) of that proof previously appealed to it implicitly), concentrating the curve-specific (more precisely, smooth-proper-geom-irr-specific) input at a named lemma the iter-146 KDM ring-side prover lane can dispatch as a black box. The KDM lemma's signature is left at its current finite-type-$k$-algebra generality; consumers (notably \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}) supply the chart-of-proper-curve hypothesis at their site by invoking this lemma where the descent step requires it.
2103	\end{lemma}

2105	\begin{proof}
2106	  \uses{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field}
2107	  By \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} the global-section ring $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ coincides with the image of the canonical $\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$; in particular $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ is a copy of $k$ inside $X$'s global structure sheaf.
2108	
2109	  The chart restriction $\rho_V \colon \Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) \to B = \Gamma(X, V)$ is the canonical sheaf-restriction map. On a reduced source (smoothness of $X$ over $k$ implies reducedness, Mathlib's \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.SmoothScheme}-namespace lemmas combined with the standard reducedness-from-regular-local-rings instance), the structure-sheaf restriction along an open immersion to a non-empty affine open is injective (sheaf separatedness on a reduced source: \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsOpenImmersion} family + \texttt{Scheme.Spec\_germ\_injective}-shape lemmas). Hence $\rho_V$ embeds the field $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X) = k$ inside $B$ as $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$.
2110	
2111	  For the integral-closure property: suppose $b \in B$ is integral over $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$, i.e.\ satisfies $b^{n} + a_{n-1} b^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0 = 0$ in $B$ with each $a_i \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$. Smoothness plus geometric irreducibility of $X$ over $k$ gives $X$ integral (smooth $\Rightarrow$ reduced; geometrically irreducible $\Rightarrow$ irreducible), so the affine chart $V = \Spec B$ embeds into the function field $K(X)$ of $X$ via the canonical generic-point localisation (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField}; an open immersion of integral schemes induces an injection on rings into $K(X)$). The same monic relation holds in $K(X)$, with coefficients $a_i \in k \subseteq K(X)$, so $b$ is integral over $k$ inside $K(X)$. The integral closure of $k$ in $K(X)$ is the field of constants of $X$, which equals $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$ for $X$ smooth proper geometrically irreducible over $k$ (a standard fact, Stacks Tag~0BUG; the integral elements over $k$ in $K(X)$ correspond to regular functions on $X$, which by properness are global sections, which by \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} are exactly $k$). Hence $b \in k$ inside $K(X)$, and pulling back along $\rho_V$ exhibits $b \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$.
2112	
2113	  Estimated build: $\sim 80$--$150$ LOC. The dominant cost is the chart-to-function-field embedding $B \hookrightarrow K(X)$ (an open-immersion-induced localisation idiom; chase via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Hom.appLE} on the generic point) and the "integral closure of $k$ in $K(X)$ equals $\Gamma(X, \mathcal O_X)$" identification (a thin wrapper around the constants-vanishing of \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} plus the standard normality-of-smooth-schemes fact).
2114	
2115	  \emph{Mathlib status.} The chart-of-reduced-source injectivity for $\rho_V$ is [verified] via \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.IsOpenImmersion}-namespace lemmas on the structure sheaf. The function-field embedding $B \hookrightarrow K(X)$ for $B$ an affine chart of an integral scheme is [verified in idiom] in \texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.FunctionField}. The "integral closure of $k$ in $K(X)$ equals constants" identification for a smooth proper geometrically irreducible scheme is the standard Stacks Tag~0BUG; in Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} it is [gap]-fill via a thin in-tree wrapper that chains \cref{lem:constants_integral_over_base_field} with the normality-of-smooth-$k$-schemes instance (\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Smooth.normal}).
2116	\end{proof}

2118	\begin{theorem}\leanok
2119	[(ii-$\beta$-core ring-side) Kernel of the universal K\"ahler derivation: $D b = 0$ implies $b$ in the image of the base field]
2120	  \label{lem:KaehlerDifferential_mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}
2121	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.KaehlerDifferential.mem_range_algebraMap_of_D_eq_zero}
2122	  \uses{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product, lem:KaehlerDifferential_constants_in_chart_of_proper_curve}
2123	  Let $k$ be a field and $B$ a commutative $k$-algebra of finite type (or, more generally, a $k$-algebra satisfying the standard-smooth chart hypothesis used by piece (i) — finitely generated and standard-smooth-of-relative-dimension $n$). Let $D \colon B \to \Omega_{B/k}$ denote the universal K\"ahler derivation (Mathlib's \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.D} as a $B$-linear additive Leibniz-rule map). Then for every $b \in B$,
2124	  \[
2125	    D b \;=\; 0 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad b \;\in\; \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B).
2126	  \]
2127	\end{theorem}
2128	
2129	\begin{proof}
2130	  \uses{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product, lem:KaehlerDifferential_constants_in_chart_of_proper_curve}
2131	  The proof presents two alternative attack routes for the forward direction ``$D b = 0 \Rightarrow b \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$''; the eventual Lean end-state is a case-split body \texttt{if CharZero k then (p2) else (p1)}. The base inclusion ``$b \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B) \Rightarrow D b = 0$'' is the standard \texttt{Derivation.map\_algebraMap} (Mathlib, \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Derivation.Basic}) and supplies the reverse direction independently of either route. The iter-147 prover lane identified (p2) as a standalone viable characteristic-$0$ first-attempt route (not nested under a characteristic-$p$ Frobenius wrapper as in the iter-145 framing); the iter-148+ prover lane may attempt (p2) first because the typeclass bridge is well-understood and the (p1) characteristic-$p$ path additionally requires the chart-of-proper-curve helper to be scaffolded.
2132	
2133	  \emph{Primary path (p2) --- characteristic-$0$ via \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants}.} When $\mathtt{CharZero}\,k$ (or more generally when the chart-algebra has trivial $p$-torsion in its K\"ahler differentials --- so that the integer coefficient $\alpha_i$ in the $D$-expansion of (p1.b) below never vanishes), the kernel of $D \colon B \to \Omega_{B/k}$ is exactly $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$. The Mathlib idiom is the \texttt{Differential.ContainConstants} typeclass (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Derivation.DifferentialRing}, lines 62--70; one Mathlib consumer at \texttt{FieldTheory.Differential.Liouville}): a differential ring satisfies \texttt{ContainConstants} when the kernel of the chosen derivation equals the image of its constant subring. The typeclass is positioned for a specific derivation $B \to B$ (a \texttt{Differential B} instance), not the universal K\"ahler derivation $D \colon B \to \Omega_{B/k}$, so the bridge requires packaging $D$ through a chosen basis of $\Omega_{B/k}$. Concretely: the standard-smooth chart hypothesis on $B$ (supplied via the chart-side \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension} structure assembled out of \cref{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product}) makes $\Omega_{B/k}$ free of finite rank $n$ via \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}); fix a basis $\{\mathrm dx_1, \ldots, \mathrm dx_n\}$ of $\Omega_{B/k}$, project $D$ to each basis-coefficient $\partial_i \colon B \to B$ ($D b = \sum_i (\partial_i b)\, \mathrm dx_i$), and use each $\partial_i$ as a \texttt{Differential B} instance. In characteristic $0$ the \texttt{ContainConstants} property holds for each $\partial_i$, so $D b = 0 \Rightarrow (\forall i)\,\partial_i b = 0 \Rightarrow b \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ directly, with no Frobenius detour. This path does \emph{not} consume \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_constants_in_chart_of_proper_curve}: the typeclass closes the forward direction by itself, eliminating the chart-of-proper-curve dependency that the (p1) char-$p$ alternative carries. Estimated bridge build: $\sim 80$--$150$ LOC.
2134	
2135	  \emph{Alternative path (p1) --- characteristic-$p$ via standard-smooth chart presentation.} When $\mathtt{CharP}\,k\,p$ for $p > 0$, the kernel of $D$ is strictly larger than $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ in general: any $b = c^p$ with $c \in B$ has $D(c^p) = p c^{p-1} D c = 0$, so $\ker D \supseteq B^p$ (the image of the absolute Frobenius on $B$). The recovery follows the Cartier-direction characterisation $\ker D = B^p$ on a standard-smooth chart (Stacks Tag~07F4), then a Frobenius-power iteration, then descent through the chart-of-proper-curve integrally-closed-constants helper. Since Mathlib snapshot \texttt{b80f227} does not ship the chart-level $\ker D = B^p$ as an off-the-shelf lemma for the standard-smooth-chart case, the project's in-tree build decomposes the chart-level step into four sub-steps (p1.a)--(p1.d), followed by the Frobenius-iteration step (p1.e) and the chart-of-proper-curve descent (p1.f):
2136	  \begin{itemize}
2137	    \item[(p1.a)] \emph{Subring structure of $\ker D$.} The kernel $\ker D \subseteq B$ is closed under addition (\texttt{KaehlerDifferential.D\_add} of \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Kaehler.Basic}: $D(b_1 + b_2) = D b_1 + D b_2$) and under multiplication via the Leibniz rule (\texttt{Derivation.leibniz} / \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.D\_mul}: $D(b_1 b_2) = b_1 \cdot D b_2 + b_2 \cdot D b_1$; when both factors lie in $\ker D$, the right-hand side vanishes). It contains $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ (\texttt{Derivation.map\_algebraMap}). Hence $\ker D$ is a $k$-sub-algebra of $B$ --- a \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.D\_ker\_subring}-shape statement, assemblable from the two named Mathlib lemmas plus the standard \texttt{Subring}-builder boilerplate of \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Subring.Basic}.
2138	    \item[(p1.b)] \emph{Standard-smooth chart presentation and $D$-coefficient calculus.} The standard-smooth-of-relative-dimension-$n$ chart hypothesis (\texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension} of \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmooth}, instantiated on the chart of \cref{lem:chart_algebra_isPushout_of_affine_product}) supplies a presentation $B \cong k[x_1, \ldots, x_n, y_1, \ldots, y_m] / (f_1, \ldots, f_m)$ with the Jacobian $\det(\partial f_i / \partial y_j)$ invertible in $B$. The universal K\"ahler module $\Omega_{B/k}$ is free of rank $n$ on the basis $\{\mathrm dx_1, \ldots, \mathrm dx_n\}$ (\texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} of \texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Smooth.StandardSmoothCotangent}; the rank-$n$ identification via \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmoothOfRelativeDimension.rank\_kaehlerDifferential}). For any $b = \sum_{\alpha \in \mathbb N^n} c_\alpha\, x^\alpha\;+\;(\text{relations})$, the universal derivation expands as
2139	    \[
2140	      D b \;=\; \sum_{\alpha} c_\alpha\!\!\sum_{i=1}^{n} \alpha_i\, x_i^{\alpha_i - 1}\!\!\prod_{j \neq i} x_j^{\alpha_j}\, \mathrm dx_i \qquad \pmod{\mathrm df_1, \ldots, \mathrm df_m}.
2141	    \]
2142	    In characteristic $p$, the integer coefficient $\alpha_i$ vanishes precisely when $p \mid \alpha_i$. Modulo the standard-smooth relations (the Jacobian condition expresses each $\mathrm dy_j$ as a $B$-linear combination of the $\mathrm dx_i$'s, eliminating the $\mathrm dy_j$'s coherently), $D b = 0$ unpacks via the freeness of the $\{\mathrm dx_i\}$-basis into the coefficient-wise system $\alpha_i\, c_\alpha = 0$ for every $\alpha$ and every $i$.
2143	    \item[(p1.c)] \emph{Conclusion: $b$ is a polynomial in $x_i^p$ modulo relations.} The system $\alpha_i\, c_\alpha = 0$ in characteristic $p$ forces $c_\alpha = 0$ unless every $\alpha_i$ is divisible by $p$. Writing $\alpha_i = p \beta_i$, the polynomial unpacks as
2144	    \[
2145	      b \;=\; \sum_{\beta \in \mathbb N^n} c_{p \beta}\, x^{p \beta} \;+\; (\text{relations}) \;=\; \sum_{\beta} c_{p \beta}\,(x^{\beta})^{p} \;+\; (\text{relations}).
2146	    \]
2147	    Freeness of $\Omega_{B/k}$ on $\{\mathrm dx_i\}$ from (p1.b) makes the coefficient-extraction step rigorous: separating the $\mathrm dx_i$-components of $D b$ shows that $D b = 0$ is genuinely the conjunction of the per-$\mathrm dx_i$ component vanishings, not a coincidental cancellation across $i$.
2148	    \item[(p1.d)] \emph{Lift back to $b \in B^p$.} The polynomial relation $b = \sum_\beta c_{p \beta}\,(x^\beta)^{p}\,\pmod{\text{relations}}$ exhibits $b$ as a $p$-th power up to the standard-smooth relations. Setting $c := \sum_\beta c_{p \beta}^{(p)} \cdot x^\beta + (\text{relations})$, where $c_{p \beta}^{(p)} \in B$ is a $p$-th root of $c_{p \beta}$ supplied at the chart-coefficient level (the coefficients $c_{p \beta} \in k \cdot (\text{constants part of } B)$ admit $p$-th roots via the Frobenius-iteration depth bound of (p1.e) below, propagating to the constants part of $B$, where the absolute Frobenius is the identity on the $p$-th-power-closed constants; for $k$ perfect this is direct, and for non-perfect $k$ the (p1.e) depth absorbs the residual data), one verifies $c^{p} = b$ modulo the relations via the characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-power expansion. The expansion uses that the iterated Frobenius is a ring homomorphism commuting with $\mathtt{algebraMap}$, packaged in Mathlib as \texttt{RingHom.iterateFrobenius\_comm} (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius}): this single lemma supplies the additivity + multiplicativity of $\mathrm{Frobenius}$ (and of every iterate $\mathrm{Frobenius}^{e}$) needed to compute $c^p = \sum_\beta c_{p \beta}\,(x^\beta)^{p}$, matching $b$ up to relations. Together with the free-module structure $\Omega_{B/k} \cong B^{n}$ from \texttt{Algebra.IsStandardSmooth.free\_kaehlerDifferential} (which guarantees the coefficient-extraction is unambiguous), this delivers $b \in B^p$. The cleanest in-tree concretisation pattern follows Stacks Tag~07F4 (Cartier-direction $\ker D = B^p$ for a smooth $k$-algebra in characteristic $p$); the explicit construction of $c$ is the contribution of this sub-step. Estimated build for the (p1.a)--(p1.d) chain: $\sim 60$--$100$ LOC.
2149	    \item[(p1.e)] \emph{Frobenius iteration via \texttt{RingHom.iterateFrobenius\_comm}.} Mathlib's \texttt{RingHom.iterateFrobenius\_comm} (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.CharP.Frobenius}) gives the commutation $\mathrm{Frobenius}^n \circ f = f \circ \mathrm{Frobenius}^n$ for any ring map $f \colon B \to B$ in characteristic $p$. Applied iteratively on the chain $b = c_1^p$ from (p1.d) (with $D c_1$ being zero modulo $B^p$ by re-running the (p1.a)--(p1.d) chain on the corresponding K\"ahler derivation of $c_1$), we obtain a chain $b = c_1^p = c_2^{p^2} = \cdots = c_e^{p^e}$ of $p^e$-th roots, all lying in $B$. The chain stabilises in finitely many steps because $B$ is a finitely-generated $k$-algebra with a standard-smooth presentation of relative dimension $n$ from piece (i); the Frobenius depth $e$ is uniformly bounded by the chart's structural data. Estimated build: $\sim 40$--$80$ LOC.
2150	    \item[(p1.f)] \emph{Descent to $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ via the chart-of-proper-curve integrally-closed-constants helper.} At chart-uniform Frobenius depth $e$, $b = c_e^{p^e}$ with $c_e \in B$ and $D c_e = 0$ modulo the iterated reduction. The chart-uniform descent forces $c_e$ to be integral over $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ inside $B$ (via the iter-on-(p1.a)--(p1.d) inductive base: at each Frobenius layer the kernel-of-$D$ data confines the lifted-$c_e$ to algebraic dependence on the $k$-image inside the chart). By \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_constants_in_chart_of_proper_curve} --- invoked on $V := \Spec B$ viewed as an affine chart of the smooth proper geometrically irreducible ambient scheme supplied by the consumer (see consumer block \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart} Step 4 for the chart-of-proper-curve hypothesis instantiation; the consumer carries the ambient-scheme data, which this proof step inherits as a standing chart-side premise) --- $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ is integrally closed in $B$. Hence $c_e \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$, and Frobenius-stability of $\operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$ (the Frobenius of a field of characteristic $p$ lands inside the image of the prime field, hence inside the image of $k$) gives $b = c_e^{p^e} \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,B)$. Estimated build: $\sim 40$--$50$ LOC (the bulk concentrated in \cref{lem:KaehlerDifferential_constants_in_chart_of_proper_curve}).
2151	  \end{itemize}
2152	  Composing (p1.a)--(p1.f) closes the characteristic-$p$ alternative path. Estimated build for (p1) as a whole: $\sim 140$--$230$ LOC, dominated by the (p1.a)--(p1.d) chain ($\sim 60$--$100$ LOC), the Frobenius iteration (p1.e) ($\sim 40$--$80$ LOC), and the chart-of-proper-curve descent (p1.f) ($\sim 40$--$50$ LOC). Mathlib's existing \texttt{Algebra.IsSeparable.iff\_isPurelyInseparable\_extension} delivers only the separable-direction $\ker D = B^p$ (too restrictive); the (p1.a)--(p1.d) chain above is the standard-smooth-direction version.
2153	
2154	  \emph{Closure end state and ordering.} The eventual Lean body is the case-split \texttt{if CharZero k then (p2) else (p1)}: the (p2) typeclass bridge closes the characteristic-$0$ branch outright (no Frobenius detour, no chart-of-proper-curve helper); the (p1) Cartier-direction chain closes the characteristic-$p$ branch via the sub-step chain above. The (p1) and (p2) sub-paths are independent and may be formalised in parallel; their individual sub-substeps are independently Mathlib-contribution-grade.

2203	  The proof packages the chart-algebra ($\beta$) chain into the iter-125-refactored \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} idiom. Three steps:
2204	
2205	  \emph{Step 1: reduce $\mathrm df = \mathrm dg$ to a single difference morphism $h$ with $\mathrm dh = 0$.} Using the group structure of $A$, define $h := \mu \circ \langle f, \iota \circ g \rangle \colon C \to A$. The K\"ahler-derivation additivity (Mathlib's \texttt{KaehlerDifferential.D\_sub} / \texttt{Derivation.map\_sub} on the algebra side; lifted to the scheme level via the same functorial chain used in \cref{lem:GrpObj_mulRight_globalises}) gives $\mathrm dh = \mathrm df - \mathrm dg = 0$. The agreement-on-$U$ hypothesis transports to $h|_{U} = \eta_A \circ \pi_C|_{U}$ (i.e.\ on $U$, $h$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$), because $f|_U = g|_U$ implies $\mu(f, \iota \circ g)|_U = \mu(g, \iota \circ g)|_U = \eta_A \circ \pi_C|_U$ by the group identity $b \cdot b^{-1} = \eta_A$ (Mathlib's $\GrpObj$ axiom \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mul\_inv\_self} or the equivalent \texttt{lift\_comp\_inv\_*} pattern).
2206	
2207	  \emph{Step 2: apply the per-chart helper \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart} chart-by-chart on $C$.} For each affine chart $V = \Spec R \subseteq C.\mathrm{left}$ on which the restriction of $h$ lands inside a chart $W = \Spec B$ of $A.\mathrm{left}$ around the image $h(V) \subseteq W$ (such chart pairs exist by quasi-compactness and the standard-smooth presentation of $A.\mathrm{hom}$; chart-uniform existence is automatic from the smoothness of $A.\mathrm{hom}$ on each fibre), the chart-restriction of $h$ is a $k$-algebra map $h^{\sharp} \colon B \to R$ with $\mathrm dB(h^{\sharp}(b)) = 0$ globally (\cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart} consumes the global Step 3 of its proof here). The helper concludes that $h^{\sharp}(b) \in \operatorname{range}(\mathtt{algebraMap}\,k\,R)$ for every $b \in B$, i.e.\ $h^{\sharp}$ factors as $B \to k \to R$. Assembling chart-by-chart via sheaf compatibility, the scheme-morphism $h \colon C \to A$ factors through $\Spec k \to A$ globally: $h = c' \circ \pi_C$ for a unique constant morphism $c' \colon \Spec k \to A$ determined by the chart-factorisation's image.
2208	
2209	  \emph{Step 3: identify the constant value via Step 1's agreement on $U$.} From Step 1's agreement $h|_{U} = \eta_A \circ \pi_C|_{U}$ and Step 2's factorisation $h = c' \circ \pi_C$, the constant value satisfies $c' \circ \pi_C|_{U} = \eta_A \circ \pi_C|_{U}$, hence $c' = \eta_A$ (the structure map $\pi_C|_{U} \colon U \to \Spec k$ is non-empty since $U$ is non-empty, so the equation on $U$ pins $c'$). Therefore $h = \eta_A \circ \pi_C$ as a morphism $C \to A$, i.e.\ $h$ is the constant morphism at $\eta_A$. Inverting Step 1's construction $h = \mu \circ \langle f, \iota \circ g \rangle$ gives the cancellation $\mu(f, \iota \circ g) = \eta_A \circ \pi_C \Rightarrow f = g$ via the $\GrpObj$ cancellation identity (Mathlib's \texttt{CategoryTheory.GrpObj.mul\_inv\_eq\_iff} or the equivalent \texttt{lift\_comp\_inv\_*} chain on $\GrpObj A$).
2210	
2211	  Finally, the conclusion is packaged via the iter-125-refactored \cref{thm:GrpObj_eq_of_eqOnOpen} (project lemma \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen}, \texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Rigidity.lean}): on a non-empty open $U \subseteq C$ where $f|_{U} = g|_{U}$ (supplied by the agreement hypothesis, or by promoting the pointing $f(p) = g(p)$ to a chart around $p$ via continuity), since $C$ is reduced (smooth) and $A$ is separated (proper), the scheme-morphism equality $f = g$ globally follows.
2212	
2213	  Estimated build: $\sim 100$--$150$ LOC. The chart-cover assembly of Step 2 is the dominant cost ($\sim 60$--$90$ LOC); Steps 1 and 3 are $\sim 20$--$30$ LOC each.

The chart-by-chart sheaf-assembly of Step 2 reuses the standard pattern already exercised by the project's \texttt{Scheme.Over.ext\_of\_eqOnOpen} body; the chart-factorisation data is supplied directly by \cref{lem:chart_algebra_df_zero_factors_through_constant_on_chart}.

53	
54	# Blueprint Writer
55	
56	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
57	
58	## Your Job
59	
60	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
61	
62	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
63	
64	## Directive Format
65	
66	```markdown
67	# Blueprint Writer Directive
68	
69	## Slug
70	<slug>
71	
72	## Target chapter
73	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
74	
75	## Strategy context
76	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
77	
78	## Required content
79	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
80	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
81	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
82	- ...
83	
84	## Out of scope
85	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
86	
87	## References
88	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
89	- ...
90	
91	## Expected outcome
92	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
93	```
94	
95	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
96	
97	## Chapter format
98	
99	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
100	
101	```latex
102	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
103	  \label{thm:some_label}
104	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
105	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
106	  Informal statement of the theorem, using standard mathematical notation.
107	\end{theorem}
108	
109	\begin{proof}
110	  \uses{thm:another_result}
111	  Step-by-step informal proof. Reference blueprint labels with \uses{...}
112	  so the dependency graph stays accurate.
113	\end{proof}
114	```
115	
116	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
117
